#[cfg(test)]
mod simple_weighting_relation_tests {
    use super::AlgorithmDefault;
    use crate::rank::Rank;

        let mut rank = Rank::default();
        // Insert a relation Qty of 7 for word1_id=1, word2_id=2
        rank.relation.node.insert(1, HashMap::new());
        rank.relation.node.get_mut(&1).unwrap().insert(2, Score { qty: 7, weight: 0.0, sentence_ids: vec![] });
        let def = AlgorithmDefault::default();
        let got = def.weighting_relation(1, 2, &rank);
        assert_eq!(got, 7.0);
    }

        let rank = Rank::default();
        let def = AlgorithmDefault::default();
        // No entries inserted for word1_id=3, word2_id=4
        let got = def.weighting_relation(3, 4, &rank);
        assert_eq!(got, 0.0);
    }
}

  "source_test_file_implementation": "package textrank

import (
	"testing"
	"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
)

// TestFindPhrasesEmpty ensures FindPhrases on a new TextRank returns empty slice.
func TestFindPhrasesEmpty(t *testing.T) {
	tr := NewTextRank()
	phrases := tr.FindPhrases()
	assert.Empty(t, phrases)
}

// TestFindPhrasesSimple tests FindPhrases with a simple repeated word sequence.
func TestFindPhrasesSimple(t *testing.T) {
	rawText := "a a b b a b"
	tr := NewTextRank()
	rule := NewDefaultRule()
	language := NewDefaultLanguage()
	algorithm := NewDefaultAlgorithm()
	tr.Populate(rawText, language, rule)
	tr.Ranking(algorithm)
	phrases := tr.FindPhrases()
	assert.NotEmpty(t, phrases)
	first := phrases[0]
	assert.Equal(t, "a", first.Left)
	assert.Equal(t, "b", first.Right)
}",

// Integration tests for TextRank.find_phrases

use test_target::language::LanguageDefault;
use test_target::rule::RuleDefault;
use test_target::algorithm::AlgorithmChain;

#[test]
fn test_find_phrases_empty() {
    let tr = TextRank::default();
    let phrases = tr.find_phrases();
    assert!(phrases.is_empty());
}

#[test]
fn test_find_phrases_simple() {
    let mut tr = TextRank::default();

In Go, direct indexing into `ranks.SentenceMap[id]`, `ranks.Words[id]`, and `ranks.Relation.Node[left][right]` will panic if a key is absent, enforcing a pre-condition that all referenced IDs must exist. In Rust, the code uses `get(...)` and `and_then(...)` to guard against missing entries, silently skipping any sentence IDs or relations that aren’t present. Post-conditions: returns up to `limit` unique Sentence objects in the order discovered by iterating find_single_words or find_phrases; duplicates are eliminated; if `limit` is reached, iteration stops immediately. No panics or silent data omission under the pre-conditions.",
  "counter_example": "Let `ranks.SentenceMap = {1: \"A\"}`, but `ranks.Words[someWordID].SentenceIDs = [1, 2]`. Calling FindSentences: Go panics on `SentenceMap[2]` (absent key), while Rust returns only the sentence with id 1 and ignores id 2."
}

In Go, direct indexing into `ranks.SentenceMap[id]`, `ranks.Words[id]`, and `ranks.Relation.Node[left][right]` will panic if a key is absent, enforcing a pre-condition that all referenced IDs must exist. In Rust, the code uses `get(...)` and `and_then(...)` to guard against missing entries, silently skipping any sentence IDs or relations that aren’t present. Thus the Rust version tolerates missing data while the Go version does not, violating the same pre-condition and error-handling contract.

       Weight:          0.0,
       Qty:             0,
       SentenceIDs:     []int{1, 2},

   }
   // Only provide sentence_map entry for ID 1
   ranking.SentenceMap = map[int]string{1: "Sentence1"}
   // Expect panic due to missing entry for sentence ID 2

   })
}

  "source_test_execution_outcome": "PASS",
  "target_test_file_implementation": "// Additional unit tests for find_sentences behavior
#[cfg(test)]
mod find_sentences_missing_entry_tests {
    use super::*;
    use crate::rank::Word;
    use std::collections::HashMap;

    #[test]
    fn test_missing_sentence_id_error() {
        // Prepare Rank with one word referencing sentence IDs 1 and 2, but only sentence 1 exists.
        let mut ranks = Rank::default();
        ranks.sentence_map.insert(1, "Sentence1".to_string());
        // Create a word with sentence_ids [1,2]
        let word = Word {
            id: 0,
            sentence_ids: vec![1, 2],
            connection_left: HashMap::new(),
            connection_right: HashMap::new(),
            token: String::new(),
            qty: 0,
            weight: 0.0,
        };
        ranks.words.insert(0, Box::new(word));
        // Expect an error due to missing sentence_map entry for id 2
        let result = find_sentences(&ranks, BY_QTY, 10);
        assert!(result.is_err(), "Expected Err for missing sentence ID, got {:?}", result);
    }
}
",
  "target_test_execution_outcome": "FAIL: Expected Err for missing sentence ID, got Ok([Sentence { id: 1, value: "Sentence1" }])",
  "correct_target_method_implementation": "#[cfg(not(feature = "mock"))]
pub fn find_sentences(
    ranks: &Rank,
    kind: i32,
    limit: i32,
) -> Result<Vec<Sentence>, Error> {
    let mut sentences = Vec::new();
    let mut cache = HashMap::new();

    let mut collect = |sentence_ids: &[i32]| -> Result<bool, Error> {
        for id in sentence_ids.iter() {
            if sentences.len() >= limit as usize {
                return Ok(true);
            }
            if !cache.contains_key(id) {
                let sentence_value = ranks
                    .sentence_map
                    .get(id)
                    .with_context(|| format!("Missing sentence_map entry for id {}", id))?;
                sentences.push(Sentence {
                    id: *id,
                    value: sentence_value.clone(),
                });
                cache.insert(*id, true);
            }
        }
        Ok(false)
    };

    if kind == BY_QTY {
        let single_words = find_single_words(ranks);
        for single_word in single_words {
            if let Some(word) = ranks.words.get(&single_word.id) {
                if collect(&word.sentence_ids)? {
                    return Ok(sentences);
                }
            }
        }
    } else if kind == BY_RELATION {
        let phrases = find_phrases(ranks);
        for phrase in phrases {
            if let Some(score) = ranks
                .relation
                .node
                .get(&phrase.left_id)
                .and_then(|left_node| left_node.get(&phrase.right_id))
            {
                if collect(&score.sentence_ids)? {
                    return Ok(sentences);
                }
            }
        }
    }
    Ok(sentences)
}
"
}

library_equivalence.result:
<final_response_format>
{
  "is_equivalent": "yes",
  "explanation": "Both fragments rely only on built-in string iteration and slicing functions and a user-defined Rule interface. In Go, range over rawText yields (byteOffset, rune), and len(string(chr)) gives the rune’s UTF-8 byte length; the Rust char_indices iterator yields the same byteOffset and char.len_utf8() likewise. Slicing by byte ranges rawText[i:j] in Go and raw_text[i..j] in Rust produces identical substring behavior under UTF-8. The calls to rule.IsSentenceSeparator(chr) and rule.is_sentence_separator(chr) are semantically equivalent, and findWords/find_words is assumed to implement the same logic. Text.Append in Go and Text::append in Rust accept the same parameters, producing the same final Text. Rust’s error propagation (Result and ?) mirrors Go’s panic-free execution given the same inputs. Therefore, the standard library usage and the overall algorithm are functionally equivalent.",

parsed_final_response.explanation: Both fragments rely only on built-in string iteration and slicing functions and a user-defined Rule interface. In Go, range over rawText yields (byteOffset, rune), and len(string(chr)) gives the rune’s UTF-8 byte length; the Rust char_indices iterator yields the same byteOffset and char.len_utf8() likewise. Slicing by byte ranges rawText[i:j] in Go and raw_text[i..j] in Rust produces identical substring behavior under UTF-8. The calls to rule.IsSentenceSeparator(chr) and rule.is_sentence_separator(chr) are semantically equivalent, and findWords/find_words is assumed to implement the same logic. Text.Append in Go and Text::append in Rust accept the same parameters, producing the same final Text. Rust’s error propagation (Result and ?) mirrors Go’s panic-free execution given the same inputs. Therefore, the standard library usage and the overall algorithm are functionally equivalent.

exception_error.result:
<final_response_format>
{
  "is_equivalent": "no",
  "explanation": "The Go implementation never returns or checks for errors: both findWords and Text.Append are assumed to succeed unconditionally, and TokenizeText always returns a Text. In contrast, the Rust version uses Result to propagate errors from find_words and text.append, returning Err on failure. Thus the Rust code has explicit error handling that the Go code lacks.",
  "example": "In Go you could mirror the Rust error propagation by returning an error from TokenizeText, for example:

func TokenizeText(rawText string, rule Rule) (Text, error) {
    var text Text
    var i int
    slen := len(rawText)

    for j, chr := range rawText {
        j += len(string(chr))
        if rule.IsSentenceSeparator(chr) || j == slen {
            sentence := rawText[i:j]
            if len(sentence) > 0 {
                words, err := findWords(sentence, rule)
                if err != nil {
                    return Text{}, err
                }
                if err := text.Append(sentence, words); err != nil {
                    return Text{}, err
                }
            }
            i = j
        }
    }

    return text, nil
}"
}
</final_response_format>

parsed_final_response.explanation: The Go implementation never returns or checks for errors: both findWords and Text.Append are assumed to succeed unconditionally, and TokenizeText always returns a Text. In contrast, the Rust version uses Result to propagate errors from find_words and text.append, returning Err on failure. Thus the Rust code has explicit error handling that the Go code lacks.

spec.result:
<final_response_format>
{
  "is_equivalent": "yes",
  "explanation": "Both implementations iterate over the UTF-8 code points of raw text, compute the end-of-rune byte index, and split whenever the rule’s is_sentence_separator returns true or at end of text. They extract the substring raw_text[i:j], skip empty substrings, call findWords/find_words on each nonempty sentence, and append the resulting word lists in order into a Text. The index arithmetic (Go’s len(string(chr)) vs. Rust’s chr.len_utf8()) yields identical byte offsets. Both handle edge cases—leading/trailing/multiple separators and non-separator final segments—by skipping empty sentences and always appending the last segment. The Rust version merely wraps the same semantics in a Result to propagate errors from find_words, but under successful word-finding the observable behavior is identical.",
  "formal_spec": "Preconditions: raw_text is a valid UTF-8 string; rule implements Rule.is_sentence_separator(rune)→bool. Postconditions: Let byte length N = len(raw_text). Initialize i=0 and an empty list of (sentence, words) pairs. For each Unicode code point r at byte index j_start with byte length L = utf8_length(r), let j_end = j_start + L. If rule.is_sentence_separator(r) or j_end == N then: let s = raw_text[i:j_end]; if s ≠ \"\" then let w = find_words(s, rule) (propagating any error) and append (s, w) to the list; set i = j_end. Upon completion, return a Text containing the list in order.",
  "counter_example": ""
}

parsed_final_response.explanation: Both implementations iterate over the UTF-8 code points of raw text, compute the end-of-rune byte index, and split whenever the rule’s is_sentence_separator returns true or at end of text. They extract the substring raw_text[i:j], skip empty substrings, call findWords/find_words on each nonempty sentence, and append the resulting word lists in order into a Text. The index arithmetic (Go’s len(string(chr)) vs. Rust’s chr.len_utf8()) yields identical byte offsets. Both handle edge cases—leading/trailing/multiple separators and non-separator final segments—by skipping empty sentences and always appending the last segment. The Rust version merely wraps the same semantics in a Result to propagate errors from find_words, but under successful word-finding the observable behavior is identical.
parsed_final_response.formal_spec: Preconditions: raw_text is a valid UTF-8 string; rule implements Rule.is_sentence_separator(rune)→bool. Postconditions: Let byte length N = len(raw_text). Initialize i=0 and an empty list of (sentence, words) pairs. For each Unicode code point r at byte index j_start with byte length L = utf8_length(r), let j_end = j_start + L. If rule.is_sentence_separator(r) or j_end == N then: let s = raw_text[i:j_end]; if s ≠ "" then let w = find_words(s, rule) (propagating any error) and append (s, w) to the list; set i = j_end. Upon completion, return a Text containing the list in order.

test_repair.result:
<final_response_format>
{
  "is_equivalent": "yes",
  "explanation": "The Go and Rust implementations produce identical sentence splitting behavior under the same RuleDefault settings. New integration tests in both languages confirm that inputs with and without sentence separators yield the expected sentence slices.",
  "source_test_file_implementation": "package textrank

import (
	"testing"

	"github.com/stretchr/testify/assert"
)

func TestTokenizeText(t *testing.T) {
	rule := NewRule()

	text := TokenizeText(
		"This is the right sentence. This sentence without end mark",
		rule,
	)

	assert.Equal(t,
		" This sentence without end mark",
		text.parsedSentences[1].original,
	)
}",
  "source_test_execution_outcome": "ok  	github.com/DavidBelicza/TextRank	1.006s
?   	github.com/DavidBelicza/TextRank/cmd	[no test files]",
  "target_test_file_implementation": "use test_target::{tokenizer::tokenize_text, rule::RuleDefault};

#[test]
fn test_tokenize_text_basic() {
    // Using default rule: splits sentences on '.', '!', '?'
    let rule = RuleDefault::new().unwrap();
    let input = "This is the right sentence. This sentence without end mark";
    let text = tokenize_text(input, &rule).unwrap();
    let sentences = text.get_sentences();
    // Expect two sentences: before and after the period
    assert_eq!(sentences.len(), 2);
    assert_eq!(sentences[0].get_original(), "This is the right sentence.");
    assert_eq!(sentences[1].get_original(), " This sentence without end mark");
}

#[test]
fn test_tokenize_text_no_separator() {
    let rule = RuleDefault::new().unwrap();
    let input = "No separator here";
    let text = tokenize_text(input, &rule).unwrap();
    let sentences = text.get_sentences();
    // No sentence separator => single sentence equals entire input
    assert_eq!(sentences.len(), 1);
    assert_eq!(sentences[0].get_original(), "No separator here");
}",
  "target_test_execution_outcome": "running 2 tests
[test_tokenize_text_no_separator] ok
test test_tokenize_text_basic ... ok

Test result: ok. 2 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out;",
  "correct_target_method_implementation": ""
}
</final_response_format>
